title: Earth Science
intro: Questions on the Earth Science unit are dominated by surface and structural processes: glaciers are the single most frequent answerline, and basalt, soil, quartz, and salinity all recur heavily, reflecting a canon built around mineralogy, geomorphology, and the physical properties of Earth materials. Plate tectonics and Earth's interior form a closely related cluster — earthquakes, magma, metamorphism, seafloor spreading, subduction, and the mantle recur constantly, often tied to the named evidence (geomagnetic reversal, the Vine-Matthews-Morley hypothesis, mid-ocean ridges) that established the theory.

Atmospheric science and oceanography supply a second major bloc of answers. Tides, ocean currents, upwelling, and thermohaline circulation appear alongside atmospheric topics like jet streams, the Coriolis effect, lapse rate, and specific storm types (supercells, tornadoes, hurricanes), with named circulation features like El Nino, the Madden-Julian oscillation, and the Walker circulation recurring as more specific answers. Climate and paleoclimate contribute their own cluster — ice cores, Milankovitch cycles, ocean acidification, and radiative forcing — distinct from day-to-day meteorology but drawing on the same physical vocabulary.

Historical geology rounds out the canon with named periods and events: the Carboniferous, Permian, and Triassic periods, the Cambrian explosion, and the Permian-Triassic extinction all recur as answers keyed to their defining fossil record or environmental change, alongside dating methods like carbon-14 and tree rings. A smaller set of answerlines are the scientists and named hypotheses that structured the field — John Tuzo Wilson's transform faults, Darcy's law, and the uniformitarianism principle — typically appearing as common links tying together the mechanical and observational evidence that built the theory of plate tectonics.

sections.name: Mineralogy & Petrology
sections.blurb: Minerals and the igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic rocks they form.

entries.note: Fine-grained, mafic volcanic rock forming oceanic crust and lava flows, rich in iron and magnesium.
Common silicate mineral (SiO2) with a Mohs hardness of 7, a major component of sandstone and granite.
Abundant element forming Earth's core and key minerals like magnetite and hematite.
Process altering rock's mineral composition and texture under heat and pressure without melting.
Most abundant mineral group in Earth's crust, key components of granite and basalt.
Compound forming limestone and marine shells, central to the ocean's carbonate system and acidification concerns.
Sedimentary rock composed mainly of calcium carbonate, often formed from marine organism shells.
Naturally occurring volcanic glass formed from rapidly cooled felsic lava.
Fine-grained sedimentary rock formed from compacted mud and clay, often fissile.
Coarse-grained felsic igneous rock composed mainly of quartz and feldspar, forming much of continental crust.
Soft sulfate mineral (calcium sulfate dihydrate) used in plaster and drywall, forming through evaporite deposition.
Green magnesium-iron silicate mineral common in Earth's mantle and mafic igneous rocks.
Sections of oceanic crust and upper mantle thrust onto continental crust, exposing deep Earth material.
Abundant crustal metal found in minerals like feldspar and clays, extracted mainly from bauxite ore.
Fine-grained sediment composed of hydrous silicate minerals, forming through weathering and central to shale and soil.
Light metallic element abundant in mafic minerals like olivine and pyroxene.
Rocks formed from compaction and cementation of sediment, often preserving fossils and strata.
Common carbonate mineral (calcium carbonate) forming limestone and marble, easily identified by its effervescence with acid.
Mineral group containing the carbonate ion, including calcite and dolomite, forming much of Earth's sedimentary rock.
Fine-grained hydrous silicate minerals formed by weathering, central to soil and sedimentary rock formation.
Physical and chemical changes that transform loose sediment into sedimentary rock.
Rocks formed from the cooling and solidification of magma or lava.
Rock transformed from an existing rock type by heat, pressure, or chemically active fluids.
Sheet silicate minerals with perfect cleavage, common in igneous and metamorphic rocks like granite and schist.
Measure of void space within a rock or sediment, determining its capacity to hold groundwater or hydrocarbons.
Mineral (halite) formed through evaporation, dissolved in seawater and mined from evaporite deposits.
Sequence describing the order in which minerals crystallize from cooling magma, from high to low temperature.
Soft, white limestone formed from the calcite skeletons of marine microorganisms.
Fine-grained hydrous silicate minerals formed by the weathering of feldspar and other silicates.
Physical property used, though unreliably, to help identify minerals alongside streak, luster, and hardness.
Phenomenon where two or more crystals of the same mineral grow together in a symmetrical intergrowth.
Banded metamorphic rock formed under high temperature and pressure, typically from granite or sedimentary protoliths.
Describes igneous rocks and minerals rich in magnesium and iron, typically dark-colored, like basalt.
Potassium-rich feldspar mineral common in granite and a member of the Bowen's reaction series.
Group of dark, mafic silicate minerals common in basalt and other igneous rocks.
Sedimentary rock composed of cemented sand-sized mineral grains, primarily quartz.
Rock formed from compaction and cementation of sediment, often preserving fossils and strata.
Yellow, non-metallic element found near volcanic vents and hot springs, forming its own native mineral.

sections.name: Plate Tectonics & Earth's Interior
sections.blurb: The structure of Earth's interior and the tectonic forces that shape its crust.

entries.note: Sudden releases of energy along faults, generating seismic waves measured by magnitude scales.
Process by which new oceanic crust forms at mid-ocean ridges as plates diverge, key evidence for plate tectonics.
Field generated by convection in the liquid outer core, protecting Earth from solar wind and recorded in rock magnetism.
Process by which one tectonic plate slides beneath another, generating volcanism and deep earthquakes.
Areas of volcanic activity from stationary mantle plumes rising beneath moving tectonic plates, as at Hawaii and Yellowstone.
Thick layer of hot, slowly flowing rock between Earth's crust and core, driving plate motion via convection.
Underwater mountain ranges where tectonic plates diverge and new oceanic crust forms.
Mountain-building process driven by tectonic plate collisions, folding and uplifting crust.
Regions where one tectonic plate descends beneath another, producing volcanic arcs and deep-focus earthquakes.
Rigid segments of Earth's lithosphere that move atop the asthenosphere, driving continental drift.
Large ocean waves typically triggered by underwater earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, or landslides.
Fractures in rock along which displacement has occurred, sites of earthquake generation.
Upward-arching folds in rock layers formed by compressional tectonic stress.
Ductile layer of the upper mantle beneath the lithosphere, allowing tectonic plates to move.
Heat-driven circulation of mantle material thought to drive plate tectonic motion.
Change in the orientation of Earth's magnetic field, recorded in seafloor rock and used to date seafloor spreading.
Gravitational equilibrium between Earth's crust and mantle, causing crust to rise or sink in response to loading.
Theory that Earth's lithosphere is divided into moving plates whose interactions shape earthquakes, volcanism, and mountain building.
Hydrothermal vents on the seafloor emitting mineral-rich, superheated water, often near mid-ocean ridges.
Alfred Wegener's hypothesis that continents were once joined and have since moved apart, precursor to plate tectonics.
Dome-shaped intrusions where a buoyant, ductile material like salt pierces overlying rock layers.
Changes in the orientation of Earth's magnetic field, recorded in seafloor rock and used to date seafloor spreading.
Flat-topped underwater seamounts formed by erosion of extinct volcanic islands before subsidence.
Rigid outer layer of Earth comprising the crust and uppermost mantle, divided into tectonic plates.
Type of surface seismic wave causing horizontal shearing motion, generally more destructive than Rayleigh waves.
Upwellings of abnormally hot rock within the mantle thought to generate volcanic hotspots.
Boundary between Earth's crust and mantle, marked by a change in seismic wave velocity.
Thin, dense basaltic crust forming the ocean floor, continually created at mid-ocean ridges and destroyed at subduction zones.
Liquid iron-nickel layer of Earth's interior whose convective flow generates the planet's magnetic field.
Speed at which seismic waves travel through Earth's layers, used to map internal structure.
Vibrations generated by earthquakes or explosions that travel through Earth, including P, S, and surface waves.
Fractures in rock along which displacement has occurred, sites of earthquake generation.

sections.name: Volcanism
sections.blurb: Magma, lava, and the eruptive processes and landforms volcanoes produce.

entries.note: Molten rock beneath Earth's surface that erupts as lava or cools to form igneous rock.
Fragmented volcanic material ejected during an eruption, ranging from fine ash to large blocks.
Molten rock that has erupted onto Earth's surface, cooling to form extrusive igneous rock.
Expulsions of magma, gas, and ash from a volcano, ranging from effusive lava flows to explosive pyroclastic events.
Vents in volcanic regions emitting steam and gases, often the final stage of volcanic activity.
Rounded, bulbous lava formations produced by underwater volcanic eruptions.
Fast-moving, superheated current of gas and volcanic matter, one of the deadliest volcanic hazards.
Volcanic eruption style characterized by intermittent, moderate explosions of gas bubbles bursting through lava.
Openings in Earth's crust through which magma, gas, and ash erupt, building characteristic landforms.

sections.name: Geomorphology & Surface Processes
sections.blurb: Weathering, erosion, glaciation, and the shaping of Earth's surface.

entries.note: Large, slow-moving masses of ice that carve landscapes via erosion and deposit moraines.
Weathered surface layer of mineral and organic material supporting plant life, classified by horizons.
Ground that remains frozen for at least two consecutive years, common in Arctic and alpine regions.
Frozen water forming glaciers, sea ice, and permafrost, central to cryosphere processes.
Granular sediment of rock and mineral particles, forming dunes and beaches through erosion and deposition.
Mounds of wind-deposited sand, shaped by processes like saltation.
Breakdown of rock at Earth's surface via physical, chemical, or biological processes.
Landscape formed by dissolution of soluble bedrock like limestone, producing caves, sinkholes, and springs.
Natural underground cavities often formed through the dissolution of soluble rock like limestone.
Process by which sediment grains bounce and hop along a surface, driven by wind or water.
Loss of ice or snow from a glacier via melting, evaporation, or calving.
Southernmost continent, almost entirely covered by an ice sheet holding most of Earth's fresh water.
Arid regions receiving little precipitation, shaped by wind erosion and deposition.
Process by which rock and soil are worn away and transported by wind, water, or ice.
Large chunks of freshwater ice that have broken off glaciers or ice shelves to float in open water.
Fine, wind-deposited sediment forming fertile, unstratified soil layers.
Movement of sediment along a coastline caused by waves approaching at an angle.
Glacial erosion process where a glacier freezes onto rock and pulls fragments away as it moves.
Slow downslope movement of water-saturated soil, common in periglacial and permafrost regions.
Solid rock underlying loose soil, sediment, or other surface material.
Bowl-shaped hollow carved by glacial erosion at the head of a mountain valley.
Height above sea level, influencing climate, atmospheric pressure, and landscape features.
Dark organic matter in soil formed from decomposed plant and animal material.
Downslope movement of rock, soil, and debris under gravity, including landslides and mudflows.
Accumulations of rock debris deposited directly by a glacier, marking its extent or retreat.
Ice-cored mounds found in permafrost regions, formed by the freezing of groundwater beneath the surface.
Solid material transported and deposited by water, wind, or ice, the raw material of sedimentary rock.
Weathered surface layers of mineral and organic material supporting plant life, classified by horizons.

sections.name: Hydrology & Oceanography
sections.blurb: Groundwater, rivers, oceans, and the circulation of Earth's water.

entries.note: Measure of dissolved salt content in water, driving density-based ocean circulation.
Underground layers of permeable rock or sediment that store and transmit groundwater.
Periodic rise and fall of sea level caused by the gravitational pull of the Moon and Sun.
Water flowing over land after precipitation, feeding rivers and contributing to erosion.
Surface undulations of water driven by wind, generating coastal erosion and deposition.
Process bringing cold, nutrient-rich deep water to the surface, boosting marine productivity.
Large-scale movements of seawater driven by wind, density differences, and Earth's rotation.
Essential compound cycling through Earth's hydrosphere via evaporation, precipitation, and runoff.
Excessive nutrient enrichment of water bodies causing algal blooms and oxygen depletion.
Water stored beneath the surface in soil pores and rock fractures, accessed via wells and aquifers.
Flowing bodies of freshwater that shape landscapes through erosion, transport, and deposition of sediment.
Measure of water clarity affected by suspended particles, often from sediment or algal growth.
Global ocean circulation pattern driven by differences in water temperature and salinity.
Major system of ocean currents, including the Gulf Stream, redistributing heat via density-driven circulation.
Second-largest ocean, separating the Americas from Europe and Africa, home to the Gulf Stream.
Large systems of rotating ocean currents driven by wind patterns and the Coriolis effect.
Rapid increase of algae in water, often driven by nutrient pollution, that can deplete oxygen and produce toxins.
Amount of oxygen gas present in water, critical for aquatic life and affected by eutrophication.
Coastal water bodies where freshwater rivers meet and mix with ocean saltwater.
Overflow of water onto normally dry land, often caused by heavy precipitation or storm surge.
Coastal wetland ecosystems of salt-tolerant trees that stabilize shorelines and buffer storm surges.
Average height of the ocean's surface, used as a reference datum for elevation and monitored for climate-driven rise.
Large-scale movements of seawater driven by wind, density differences, and Earth's rotation.
Landforms formed where a river deposits sediment as it enters a slower-moving body of water.
Temperature of the ocean's upper layer, a key indicator for phenomena like El Nino and hurricane formation.
Unsaturated subsurface zone between the ground surface and the water table.

sections.name: Atmospheric Science & Meteorology
sections.blurb: The atmosphere's structure, weather systems, and storms.

entries.note: Fundamental atmospheric variable driving weather patterns, measured and modeled in forecasting.
Visible masses of condensed water droplets or ice crystals, classified by altitude and shape.
Narrow bands of strong westerly winds in the upper troposphere that steer weather systems.
Rate at which atmospheric temperature decreases with altitude, key to atmospheric stability.
Apparent deflection of moving objects due to Earth's rotation, shaping wind and ocean current patterns.
Apparent force causing moving objects to deflect due to Earth's rotation, shaping winds and currents.
Molecule (O3) concentrated in the stratosphere, absorbing harmful ultraviolet radiation.
Prediction of atmospheric conditions using observational data and numerical models.
Measurement of air movement rate, a key variable in storm intensity and weather prediction.
Upper atmospheric layer ionized by solar radiation, reflecting radio waves.
Severe, long-lived rotating thunderstorms capable of producing large hail and tornadoes.
Violently rotating columns of air extending from a thunderstorm to the ground.
Powerful tropical cyclones fueled by warm ocean water, forming a calm central eye surrounded by intense storms.
Sudden electrostatic discharge within a thunderstorm, occurring between clouds or between cloud and ground.
Precipitation of ice crystals, forming when atmospheric water vapor freezes directly.
Persistent easterly surface winds near the equator, part of the Hadley cell circulation.
Boundary between the troposphere and stratosphere, where temperature stops decreasing with altitude.
Lowest layer of Earth's atmosphere, where nearly all weather occurs.
Thin, wispy, high-altitude clouds composed of ice crystals.
Large-scale systems of rotating winds around a low-pressure center, including hurricanes and typhoons.
Mid-latitude atmospheric circulation cell between the Hadley and polar cells, driving variable weather patterns.
Warm, dry wind descending the leeward side of a mountain range after losing moisture on the windward side.
Cloud of water droplets suspended near the ground, reducing visibility.
Boundaries between air masses of different temperature and density, driving much of mid-latitude weather.
Soft, small ice pellets formed when supercooled water droplets freeze onto a falling snowflake.
Precipitation of ice balls formed within strong thunderstorm updrafts.
Atmospheric layer above the stratosphere where meteors burn up, the coldest layer of Earth's atmosphere.
Liquid precipitation formed when water droplets in clouds grow large enough to fall.
Large-scale electrical discharges occurring high above thunderstorms, a form of upper-atmospheric lightning.
Large-scale meanders in the jet stream and ocean currents caused by Earth's rotation, influencing weather patterns.
Meteorological scale of weather systems spanning hundreds to thousands of kilometers, like cyclones and fronts.
Uppermost atmospheric layer with extremely high temperatures, where auroras occur and satellites orbit.
Violently rotating column of air extending from a thunderstorm to the ground.
Large rotating storm systems fueled by warm ocean water, called hurricanes or typhoons regionally.
Atmospheric circulation pattern over the tropical Pacific linked to El Nino and La Nina conditions.

sections.name: Climate & Paleoclimate
sections.blurb: Climate drivers, greenhouse gases, and the record of past climate change.

entries.note: Periodic warming of central and eastern Pacific surface waters, disrupting global weather patterns.
Decrease in ocean pH from absorbing excess atmospheric CO2, threatening calcifying marine organisms.
Cylindrical samples drilled from ice sheets, preserving trapped air bubbles used to reconstruct past climate.
Eastward-moving pattern of enhanced tropical rainfall and wind that influences global weather on a 30-60 day cycle.
Measure of a surface's reflectivity, with high values like ice and snow reflecting more incoming solar radiation.
Suspended particles in the atmosphere that can cool climate by reflecting sunlight or seed cloud formation.
Greenhouse gas central to the carbon cycle and modern climate change, absorbed by oceans and plants.
Precipitation made acidic by atmospheric pollutants like sulfur dioxide and nitrogen oxides.
Potent greenhouse gas released from wetlands, livestock, and permafrost thaw, far stronger than CO2 over short timescales.
Periodic variations in Earth's orbit and axial tilt that influence long-term climate patterns and glacial cycles.
Atmospheric component produced by photosynthesis, whose rise in the Great Oxidation Event transformed early Earth's chemistry.
Measure of the change in energy balance of Earth's atmosphere caused by factors like greenhouse gases.

sections.name: Geologic Time & Historical Geology
sections.blurb: The geologic periods, mass extinctions, and dating methods that chronicle Earth's history.

entries.note: Paleozoic period (~359-299 Ma) known for extensive coal-forming swamp forests and giant arthropods.
Last Paleozoic period (~299-252 Ma), ending in the largest mass extinction in Earth's history.
Layers of sedimentary rock, whose sequence and characteristics record Earth's geologic history.
First period of the Mesozoic era (~252-201 Ma), following the Permian extinction and preceding dinosaur diversification.
Rapid diversification of complex animal life roughly 540 million years ago at the start of the Cambrian period.
First period of the Paleozoic era (~541-485 Ma), marked by the rapid diversification of animal life.
Radioactive isotope used to date organic materials up to about 50,000 years old.
Preserved remains or traces of ancient organisms, used to date rock strata and reconstruct past life.
Middle period of the Mesozoic era (~201-145 Ma), marked by dinosaur dominance and the breakup of Pangaea.
Extraterrestrial rock fragments that survive passage through the atmosphere to reach Earth's surface, some linked to mass extinctions.
The largest mass extinction in Earth's history, ending the Paleozoic era roughly 252 million years ago.
Annual growth layers in trees used to date past events and reconstruct historical climate.
Determining the age of rocks or fossils via methods like radiometric dating or stratigraphy.
Paleozoic period (~419-359 Ma) known as the "Age of Fishes," with early forests and tetrapods.
Cenozoic epoch (~56-34 Ma) marked by a warm climate and the diversification of mammals.
Epoch (~2.6 Ma to 11,700 years ago) marked by repeated glacial cycles and the appearance of early humans.
Layered structures built by ancient microbial mats, among the oldest fossil evidence of life on Earth.

sections.name: Scientists & Foundational Concepts
sections.blurb: The researchers and theories underlying modern earth science.

entries.note: Equation describing the flow rate of fluid through a porous medium, foundational to groundwater hydrology.
Canadian geophysicist who proposed transform faults and the Wilson cycle of ocean basin opening and closing.
Technique of gathering data about Earth's surface using satellite or airborne sensors rather than direct contact.
Principle that geologic processes observed today have operated similarly throughout Earth's history.
Proposal explaining symmetric magnetic stripes on the seafloor as evidence for seafloor spreading.
